steps.text: Initiate an intralaboratory method validation study.
Troubleshoot and optimize the method.
Repeat some or all performance tests.
Initiate an interlaboratory method validation study.
Troubleshoot and optimize the method after conducting a multiple laboratory study.
Clearly describe the components of a method or measurement process requiring validation.
Evaluate all process components combined during method validation efforts.
Include sampling procedures in the validation tests.
Clearly and completely state the measurement-process components validated and the combination of procedures composing a method.
Clearly define the purpose of the method and the intended use of the data.
Well define and clearly describe the method scope and applicability.

Evaluate performance characteristics including method uncertainty, bias/trueness, precision, quantification capability, detection capability, analyte concentration range, method specificity, and method ruggedness.
Validate the analytical method.
Investigate the stability of the analyte(s) from the time of sampling through the time of analysis.
Investigate the extract stability.
Address the chemical stability of the molecule rather than just the oxidation state of the radionuclide.
Report bias with an uncertainty.
Include rigorous evaluations of trueness in EPA methods.
Include rigorous evaluations of trueness in EPA method validation studies.
Evaluate trueness at the extremes of the quantitation range stated in the scope of a method, and at regulatory levels.
Evaluate method repeatability and reproducibility at the extremes of the quantitation range.
Evaluate the method uncertainty or the MQC.
Calculate the MQC of a method.
Verify the estimated MDC by testing materials containing analyte at the claimed detection level.
Evaluate method trueness and precision.
Verify MQC estimates by analyzing samples containing radionuclide at the claimed MQC level.
Verify MDC estimates by analyzing samples containing radionuclide at the claimed detection level.
Characterize analytical measurement uncertainty over an analyte concentration range.
Evaluate method ruggedness during method validation.
Evaluate method ruggedness during method validation, when practical.
Include the design and results of the interlaboratory study in the method validation summary report.
Report data from the interlaboratory study in tabular form.
Include a discussion describing the details of, and rationale for, any changes made to the method resulting from the interlaboratory study.
Peer review EPA methods of analysis in accordance with the information provided in the most current version of the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook.

preconditions.text: A detailed procedure has been written, operational parameters are believed to be optimized and finalized, and no additional changes are anticipated.
Difficulties are encountered after beginning an intralaboratory study.
Procedural changes are made that may affect the method performance characteristics.
A successful intralaboratory study has been completed.
Method performance difficulties are revealed after initiating a multiple laboratory study.
If the radiochemical method includes procedures for sampling (e.g., methods for radon in air analysis and for volatile radioactive organic compounds in soils and other solid matrices).
Ensure that proper sample preservation and storage stability studies were performed during method development.
The analysis is based on the molecular form that the radionuclide is in.
Specify the conditions changed to ensure a valid statement of reproducibility.

The laboratory must be able to predict standard uncertainties for measurements at specified levels of activity based on the same equations used to evaluate the uncertainty for each measurement result.
Evaluation of method ruggedness is practical.
Prior to publication of EPA methods of analysis

constraints.text: If procedural changes are required, the changes may affect the method performance characteristics and some or all aspects of an interlaboratory study may need to be repeated.
The chemical state of a radionuclide may be changed through oxidation-reduction reactions within the matrix if not properly preserved.
Microorganisms have the potential to oxidize or reduce certain radionuclides, and can introduce by-products that will induce chemical changes.
Spiking approach introduces uncertainty regarding the behavior of spiked materials, compared to materials containing native analyte, which may be particularly problematic for solid materials.
Consider the standard uncertainties of all input quantities (counting uncertainty, detection efficiency, chemical yields, interferences, decay corrections, sample size, etc.).
An analyte concentration range must include either a regulatory limit or a defined action level, typically near the midpoint of the range of radionuclide activity level (concentration) for a project.
